---
title: "Synergistic Mechanisms of *Aconitum carmichaelii* and *Astragalus membranaceus* Combination Against Chronic Heart Failure Due to Dilated Cardiomyopathy: Insights from Multi-Omics and Causal Inference"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
abstract: >
  The combination of the medicinal plants *Aconitum carmichaelii* (AC) and *Astragalus membranaceus* (AM) has been widely used as an adjunctive therapy for chronic heart failure (CHF) due to dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). Nevertheless, the synergistic mechanisms of the combination remain largely unexplored. In this study, we integrate network pharmacology, bioinformatics with causal inference based on multi omics, and molecular docking to systematically clarify the synergistic mechanisms of the Aconitum carmichaelii (AC) - Astragalus membranaceus (AM) combination against DCM-CHF. In total, 24 blood-absorbable active compounds are identified from this combination, and their corresponding targets are stratified into AC-specific and AM-specific subgroups with divergent functional enrichment features. Functionally, AC-related targets are primarily enriched in pathways governing myocardial contractility, ion channel activity, and mitochondrial energy metabolism, whereas AM-related targets are mainly annotated to immune modulation, inflammatory regulation, and vascular protection. Multi-organ distribution analysis demonstrates that AC targets are predominantly enriched in the heart and kidney, while AM targets are concentrated in the spleen, lung, and heart. A total of [X] core causal targets with PPH4 values greater than 0.8 are screened via Mendelian randomization and Bayesian colocalization analysis. Single-cell transcriptomic profiling identifies distinct cell-type targeting preferences of their active ingredients. AC-derived compounds preferentially target cardiomyocytes and cardiac fibroblasts, whereas AM-derived compounds primarily act on immune and endothelial cells. Opposing functional enrichment patterns are further validated by ssGSEA, with contrasting normalized enrichment score (NES) values for AC (NES = −1.52) and AM (NES = 1.83). Stable binding of neoline and calycosin to core target proteins is verified by molecular docking, with binding affinities of −8.2 kcal/mol and −7.6 kcal/mol, respectively. External dataset analysis reveals consistent differential expression trends of core targets between healthy controls and DCM-induced CHF patients, further confirming the stability and reliability of the present findings. Collectively, the AC–AM combination exerts synergistic cardioprotective effects through convergent modulation of mitochondrial energy metabolism and inflammatory signaling. Complementary therapeutic characteristics are observed, with AC specifically improving myocardial contractile performance and AM dominating immune and inflammatory regulation. This work provides a mechanistic basis for the evidence-based clinical application of the AC–AM medicinal plant combination in the treatment of DCM-induced CHF.
Keywords: chronic heart failure; dilated cardiomyopathy; network pharmacology; bioinformatics; ethnic medicinal plants; synergistic mechanisms; molecular regulatory network
---
